summary:
After removal of the urethral catheter post-hysterectomy, persistent urinary continence suggests an issue not related to sphincter control or obstruction; use of methylene blue confirmed a connection between the bladder and vagina via a fistula based solely on where it stained pads during testing. This rules out other causes such as stress incontinence due to surgical trauma but indicates need further evaluation by imaging studies specifically looking at this type of abnormality. To confirm presence/extent of any potential defect requiring repair would necessitate additional diagnostic steps including possibly ultrasound or MRI guided cystography among others depending upon local availability/experience levels within healthcare team managing case complexities arising directly from major gynecological surgeries involving pelvic organs closely positioned anatomically relative each other making precise identification crucial preoperatively if possible given nature/severity symptoms reported here today.
